actions.thought: I have been dealt Ten of Diamonds and Four of Clubs. This is an extremely weak hand with no playability or significant equity, especially in a three-handed game where position and card strength are crucial. Since I am in the Button position and the goal is to maximize my stack over the long term, folding here is the most disciplined and correct strategic play to avoid losing chips with a substandard hand.

With KQ offsuit in the small blind after the button folds, I have a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind encounter. Raising to 3 big blinds (300) is a standard play to take the initiative, build the pot, and avoid giving the big blind a free flop with random trash hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 2s, which is a very weak hand. Pro raised from the Small Blind after the Button folded. Even though I am getting a discount to call, 32o performs poorly post-flop and has very little equity against a standard opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand. Although I am on the button and could attempt a steal, folding early in the tournament with such borderline hands avoids tricky post-flop situations against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Trey of Clubs (Ts 3c), which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While I could attempt a steal or complete the blind, T3o is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Q8s is a strong hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. It has good playability and equity. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.

4c 7s is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Defending here would be a mistake as it is dominated by most of the opponent's raising range. Folding is the disciplined and optimal decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 7c, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. The Button (Flash) has raised to 3 big blinds (300 chips). Defending with Q7o in this spot is unprofitable over the long run due to its poor equity and playability post-flop, so folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am holding 9s 2s on the button in a 3-handed game. While the button is a strong position, 9-2 suited is a very weak hand with poor playability and low showdown value. Given that I am early in the tournament and there is no reason to inflate the pot with such a marginal holding, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

With J7 suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a good opportunity to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 3x asserts pressure and can win the pot uncontested right here, and if called, J7s has decent playability post-flop.

I am holding AQo in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. This is a standard spot to 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand with position throughout the post-flop play. A raise to 900 puts pressure on his opening range and allows me to define the strength of his hand while building a pot with my high equity.

J7s was a reasonable hand to open from the small blind in an attempt to steal the big blind. However, facing a 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind, my hand is simply too weak to continue. Playing a marginal suited hand out of position against an aggressive 3-bet is unprofitable. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

Ace-Queen suited is a premium hand, especially strong when opening from the Button. Raising to 3 big blinds (300) will either win the blinds immediately or build the pot in position against the blinds with a hand that plays very well post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind holding 2h 3c, which is one of the worst hands in poker. The Button (Pro) has opened with a raise to 300. Defending such a weak hand out of position is a losing play in the long run as it has very little equity and poor post-flop playability. Folding is the only sensible move here.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand (6-9 offsuit). While the pot odds are tempting (200 to win 450), 6-9 offsuit has poor equity and will be difficult to play out of position against the Button's opening range. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

On the Button with Qs 6d, I am facing the Small Blind and the Big Blind. While standard button opening ranges are wide in a three-handed game, Q6o is near the bottom of the range and generally considered a fold against competent opponents. The player in the Big Blind, Pro, has shown significant aggression in previous hands (raising in hands 1, 4, and 5), making it likely that they will either defend or 3-bet frequently. Given the high probability of being forced to play a marginal hand out of position post-flop or facing a 3-bet, I will choose the conservative path and fold this hand.

With Ace-Eight offsuit and the button having folded, I am in a favorable heads-up position against the Big Blind. Raising is the optimal play to seize initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, rather than just limping in.

Facing a Small Blind open, my AQ offsuit is a premium hand. I want to 3-bet for value against a wide SB steal range and to take the betting lead. A 3-bet to 900 is appropriately sized to apply pressure and define their range.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after I opened from the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, calling is the most sound decision. The pot odds are 2:1, and A8o holds enough equity against a standard BB 3-bet range to justify seeing a flop. A 4-bet would be over-committing with a marginal hand, and folding is too tight given the current pot size. I will call and reassess on the flop.

The board comes down King-Seven-Seven, which misses my Ace-Eight completely. My opponent, having 3-bet pre-flop, holds a range that is likely very strong, including many King-x hands or pocket pairs that dominate my current position. Betting here is futile as I have no equity, and I have no reason to bluff into a likely stronger range. Checking allows me to evaluate the opponent's intentions and potentially fold to a significant bet, or check-fold if they lead out.

On this paired King-high flop, my perceived range as the pre-flop 3-bettor hits very well, as I have all the AK, AA, and KK combos. While my actual hand (AQ) missed, a small continuation bet of around 1/3 pot will frequently fold out my opponent's middle pocket pairs (88-JJ) and missed broadways, capitalizing on my range advantage. If called, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

The board texture of King-Seven-Seven is highly favorable for my opponent who 3-bet pre-flop. My Ace-high hand with no spade draw has no showdown value and minimal equity against a range that typically includes strong Kings or pocket pairs. Calling 600 into a 2400-chip pot with such marginal holdings against a continuation bet is not mathematically sound. Therefore, the most disciplined play to protect my stack for future hands is to fold.

I am on the button with King-Ten suited, which is a strong starting hand in late position. Raising is the optimal move to take the initiative, potentially steal the blinds, or play a pot with a positional advantage. A raise to 300 is consistent with the standard betting patterns observed at the table.
83 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the button, this is a trivial fold from the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad9d, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight opened from the Button for 300. Against a wide Button opening range, Ad9d is significantly ahead. To take the initiative and extract value while protecting my hand, I will 3-bet to 1000.

I opened King-Ten suited from the button, which is a strong hand with good playability post-flop. Facing a 3-bet from the big blind (Flash), I have position and a hand that flops well. Folding is too tight, and while a 4-bet is an option, it creates a large pot with a hand that is better suited for playing in position. I will call the 3-bet to see a flop with the initiative of acting last in subsequent betting rounds.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Nines) on a board of Ac-9c-2d. This is a very strong hand given the pre-flop action where I 3-bet and Flashlight called. I need to bet for value to get paid by weaker Aces (like AJ, AT, A8s) or club draws. A bet of approximately 40% of the pot is appropriate to build the pot while keeping the opponent's range wide enough to call.

The flop comes with an Ace, which is a card that hits the Big Blind's 3-betting range extremely hard. My King-Ten high has no showdown value, no draw to a flush or straight, and minimal equity against a range that likely contains an Ace. Calling a bet here would be throwing away chips against an opponent who likely has me dominated. Given the board texture and my lack of significant equity, folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack for a better situation.

Pocket nines is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. As the first to act on the Button, I will open with a standard 3x raise to build the pot, take the initiative, and prevent the blinds from realizing cheap equity.

In a 3-handed game, King-Eight offsuit is a relatively strong hand, but playing it from the Small Blind against a Button open is challenging due to being out of position. Given that I successfully 3-bet Pro in Hand #4 and he folded, I will apply similar pressure here. A 3-bet allows me to potentially take the pot down immediately or seize the initiative if the hand proceeds to the flop. Calling would invite the Big Blind to squeeze or see a flop cheaply, which I want to avoid with a hand that plays better as an aggressor.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after an open on the Button, my hand (J8s) is dominated by the ranges typically used for 3-betting. Calling out of position with J8s is mathematically unfavorable due to the poor implied odds and the difficulty of navigating a multi-way 3-bet pot. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Flash has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively. Against a wide 3-bet range, pocket nines are strong, but 4-betting might isolate us against larger pairs or AK while folding out his bluffs. By calling in position, we keep his 3-bet bluffs in the pot, can hit a set with deep enough effective stacks, or comfortably play a low-card flop.

I hit top pair on a dry-ish board with a flush draw present. I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, and Pro called. Leading here allows me to extract value from draws (like flush draws or straight draws) and weaker hands like pocket pairs or a 6. While my kicker isn't great, top pair is very strong in a 3-bet pot 3-handed. A bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 the pot is appropriate here to maintain initiative and charge any spade draws.

Flash has shown aggression pre-flop and is continuing on a K-high board. While his sizing is on the smaller side, his 3-bet range includes many combos of Kx (like AK/KQ), higher pocket pairs (TT-AA), and strong flush draws that have plenty of equity against our 99. With only two outs to improve and being in a spot where we will likely face further aggression on the turn, folding is the most disciplined approach to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with 82o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Even with positional advantage, there is no reason to play this hand against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With 9-7 offsuit, my hand is too weak to contest the big blind, especially given that Pro has shown a willingness to raise and play aggressively. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the button position with 5-8 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with low playability. Even in a short-handed game, it is not profitable to open this hand as it lacks high-card strength and potential for strong draws. Therefore, the optimal strategy is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

With pocket deuces in the small blind after the button folds, I have a marginal but playable hand. While 22 is difficult to play post-flop out of position, open-raising puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to win the pot uncontested frequently. Flash has been aggressive with 3-bets, so if I face a reraise, I can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300 after the Button folded. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call and will have position on Pro post-flop, 96o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated and difficult to play. Given that I have a comfortable chip lead, I choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent.

King-Eight offsuit is a weak hand to open from the Button when facing an aggressive Small Blind who has demonstrated a high 3-bet frequency. Folding is the standard and safest play to avoid getting into a marginal spot with a dominated hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 6d. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). 76o is a speculative hand that plays okay in blind-vs-blind situations. Flashlight has shown a tendency to call pre-flop raises but fold to post-flop aggression. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds now or set up a post-flop play if called.

I am in the big blind with King-Eight offsuit, which is a standard hand to defend heads-up against a small blind open. Given the pot odds and my position advantage for the rest of the hand, calling is the most balanced and profitable play. I do not have enough strength to 3-bet, but folding would be too tight given the circumstances.

The flop 2c-2h-4c is a very dry board that likely missed Flashlight's calling range. Given my image as the pre-flop aggressor and Flashlight's history of folding to post-flop continuation bets in Hands #6 and #7, I will place a standard c-bet of half the pot to take down the hand immediately.

The flop is extremely dry with 2c-2h-4c. My opponent's bet of 300 into 600 offers me 3:1 pot odds, which is a profitable price to call with overcards like King-high. While I am not ahead, the small bet sizing suggests that the opponent may not have a strong hand (like a pocket pair or a deuce) and could be c-betting air. Folding here would be overly cautious, and calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn, especially since I have backdoor potential and position.

On the turn, the 8d is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have some piece of the board (a 4, a small pair, or a club draw) or an Ace-high hand they aren't ready to fold. Since I have 7-high and no real draw, I have zero showdown value. However, double-barreling into a player who has already called once on this board is risky, especially since my flop bet was relatively small and didn't elicit a fold. I will check here to see how Flashlight responds. If they check back, I might get a free card to improve or a chance to bluff the river; if they bet, I can comfortably fold my 7-high.

My pair of eights is a medium-strength hand on this dry board of 2-2-4-8. Flash's check on the turn after betting the flop indicates potential weakness. I have decent showdown value, and betting would likely only be called by better hands or cause the opponent to fold their bluffs. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot small, realize my equity, and see the river for free.

I have 7-high on a board of 2c-2h-4c-8d-Qd. After raising pre-flop and betting the flop, I checked the turn, and Flashlight checked back. This suggests Flashlight likely has a weak made hand like a small pair or Ace-high. While the Queen on the river is a potential scare card, my turn check likely capped my perceived range, making a bluff less credible. Given that I have no showdown value but want to preserve my chip lead early in the tournament, I will check and concede the pot rather than risking more chips on a low-probability bluff.

The board has paired deuces, and with my hand (Kd8c), I have a pair of eights to go with the board's deuces, resulting in two pair (eights and deuces). However, this is quite vulnerable, as any pocket pair higher than eights, any queen, or any deuce (for trips) will beat me. Flash has checked the turn and the river, which could indicate a weak hand or a trap. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me or cause him to fold bluffs. Given the risk of a check-raise and the fact that my hand is more of a bluff-catcher at this point, checking behind to go to showdown is the most sound decision.

I have pocket jacks on the button, which is a very strong starting hand. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to extract value and put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 6c5c, a hand with good post-flop playability. Facing a standard raise to 300 from the button, calling is the most efficient play to realize equity, given the pot odds and the potential for multi-way action if the big blind also calls. Folding would be too passive, and 3-betting isn't necessary here.

Facing a button raise from Flash and a call from Flashlight in the small blind, Q9 suited is a very reasonable hand to defend from the big blind. We are getting favorable pot odds (200 to win 700) and get to close the action. Our hand plays decently multiway, possessing both flush and straight potentials. I'll call to see a flop.

I am in the small blind with bottom pair on a board of Qh-8s-6d in a three-way pot. My hand has very little showdown value, and betting would be ineffective as it would likely only be called by better hands, such as a Queen, an Eight, or a higher pocket pair. The best course of action is to check, keep the pot small, and re-evaluate based on the action from the big blind and the button.

I have top pair with a 9 kicker on a Qh 8s 6d board in a 3-way pot. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash), checking is the standard play. Leading out could isolate me against stronger Queens or overpairs, while checking allows me to evaluate Flash's c-bet and Flashlight's response before committing more chips.

I have pocket Jacks, which is an overpair to the middle cards on the board but behind any Queen. Since both opponents checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, it is likely they don't have a strong hand, although a Queen is certainly in their calling ranges. I will place a continuation bet of 450 (half the pot) to extract value from middle pairs like 8x, 99, or TT, as well as straight draws like T9 or 97, and to gain information about the strength of their hands.

In a three-way pot on a rainbow board of Qh-8s-6d, my bottom pair of sixes is extremely vulnerable. The Button (Flash) bet 450 into a pot of 900, which is a standard continuation bet that could represent anything from a Queen or a pocket pair to a pure bluff. With the Big Blind (Pro) still left to act behind me, calling creates a difficult situation where I am likely behind, playing out of position, and potentially facing further action. Given the lack of strong equity and the unfavorable positioning, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice to preserve my stack.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a relatively dry, rainbow board. Flash, the pre-flop raiser on the button, is making a standard half-pot continuation bet. Against a button opening range and a c-bet, Q9s is ahead of many of his bluffs (like straight draws or random air) and is strong enough to continue. Raising would isolate us against stronger hands like AQ, KQ, or overpairs, while folding top pair here is too tight. Calling is the most sensible action, keeping his bluffing range intact while we evaluate the turn.

We have top pair with a nine kicker on a fairly dry board. The 3h on the turn doesn't change much, mostly just introducing a backdoor heart draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who continuation-bet the flop. Leading out might fold out his bluffs and isolate us against better hands like AQ, KQ, or two pair. We will check to control the pot and evaluate his turn action.

I have pocket Jacks, which is currently an underpair to the Queen on the board. Pro called my flop continuation bet and has now checked the turn. While my hand is strong, it is vulnerable to any Queen in Pro's range. By checking behind, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river without facing a potential check-raise. This also allows me to potentially induce a bluff on the river or value bet if the river is a safe card and Pro checks again.

With the turn checking through, Flash's range is fairly capped; he likely doesn't have a premium overpair or strong top pair like AQ/KQ, as those hands typically bet the turn for value and protection. His range leans towards medium pairs (TT, JJ), an 8, weaker Queens, or missed straight draws like JT. While a small value bet might get called by worse pairs, the river paired the 6 and completed a backdoor flush, making our Q9 somewhat marginal. Checking is a solid play to induce bluffs from his missed draws (like JT or 97) while getting to a safe, relatively cheap showdown against hands that would fold to a bet anyway.

I have pocket Jacks on a board of Q-8-6-3-6. While I have a strong two-pair (Jacks and Sixes), I am losing to any Queen or any Six in Pro's range. Pro called my flop bet and has checked both the turn and the river. Betting now is unlikely to get calls from worse hands like an 8 or smaller pocket pairs, and I will almost certainly be called by a Queen or better. Checking back allows me to get to showdown for free and realize the equity of my hand without risking a check-raise.

I am on the button with Q9s, a playable suited hand. Raising from the button is a standard strategy to steal the blinds and take the initiative in the hand. A raise to 300 is an appropriate opening size to put pressure on the blinds.

We are facing a 3x raise from the Button. While they likely have a wide stealing range, 6d 3d is too weak and disconnected to defend from the Small Blind out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 85 offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win 650 total), 85o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending this wide against a standard raise often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am forced to fold or bluff. Given the tournament context and stack sizes, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots.

I have King-Queen offsuit on the button. This is a strong starting hand and a standard open-raise to apply pressure on the blinds and take the initiative. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which has been the standard sizing at this table.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten-Four offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 3x the big blind. While the pot odds are okay, Ten-Four is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak holding. While I have pot odds to call, playing a weak hand out of position against a button raiser is generally a losing strategy. It is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

In a three-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit is well within the standard opening range for the button. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to pressure the blinds and leverage my position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Three suited, which is a strong hand to defend against a button open. While I am out of position, the hand has significant equity and drawing potential. Calling is the most balanced approach here to see a flop while maintaining a reasonable stack-to-pot ratio, rather than committing to a 3-bet and potentially inflating the pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. My hand, Ten-Deuce offsuit, is extremely weak and plays terribly post-flop, even getting decent odds to call. The only profitable move here is to fold.

I am out of position on a dry, paired-board texture. My hand (Ace-high with a backdoor diamond draw) has very little showdown value and no current pair. Against the pre-flop aggressor, betting or raising here would be a mistake as I would be forcing myself into a bluff with almost zero equity. I will check to see how the opponent acts; if they bet, I will likely fold, given the board hits the opening range of a button raiser quite well.

I am the pre-flop raiser and the flop comes King-high, which is a board texture that strongly favors my perceived range. Flashlight checked to me, and a continuation bet is the standard play here to maintain aggression and represent a King. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), which gives me some back-up equity. A half-pot bet should be sufficient to force a fold from many of the hands in Flashlight's small blind calling range that missed this flop.

The flop is dry and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, who has likely connected with the King. My hand is merely Ace-high with no pair and only weak backdoor possibilities. Calling a continuation bet here would be burning chips against a range that is likely beating me. Folding is the most profitable long-term strategy to preserve my stack.

The hand 7s 3s is a weak suited connector and is not worth playing from the button. Given my stack size and the strength of the cards, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited, semi-disconnected weak hand (4h 6s). With the Button folding, I would be playing out of position against the Big Blind if I continue. Given the low equity and playability of this hand, folding is the standard and optimal play.
Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with low equity and negative implied odds. Being first to act on the Button, folding is the mathematically optimal and safest play.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen of diamonds, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Since the Button has folded, it is a blind-vs-blind battle. Flashlight (Big Blind) has shown a tendency to defend their blind but fold to continuation bets on the flop. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand with initiative.

I am in the big blind with 9h6h. Flash raised to 300 from the small blind. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, which is a standard defensive play with suited connectors in position relative to the blinds. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my equity.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a paired board. Since the opponent checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to gain value from weaker Queens, pocket pairs, or club draws, and to maintain the initiative I established pre-flop.

I have 9-high with a backdoor flush draw and no pair on a paired board of 8h-Qc-8c. My hand has effectively zero showdown value and very little equity to improve. Flash is betting into me, representing at least a Queen or an 8. Continuing in this hand would be burning chips with no realistic path to winning the pot. Folding is the only disciplined play.

I am on the button with 6c 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. While opening from the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wider range, 62o is near the bottom of all possible starting hands. My recent activity has shown I'm willing to raise with strong or semi-strong hands, so I should maintain a solid image and not waste chips on trash hands like this. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Small Blind position with T7o, which is a weak hand but sufficient to complete in a heads-up scenario against the Big Blind. Since the Button folded, I only need to call 50 chips to see a flop. Completing is a low-risk way to realize equity in position against the Big Blind.

With Ace-Five offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp, checking is a solid and standard play. The hand has high-card value but a weak kicker, making it less appealing to play a bloated pot post-flop if I raise and get called. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position, keeping the variance low while retaining the post-flop positional advantage.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a dry board with a Jack as the high card. As the Small Blind, I am first to act post-flop. Given the marginal strength of my hand, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and induce bluffs or see a turn card without inflating the pot unnecessarily.

With Ace-Five offsuit on a dry flop of 7-J-3 rainbow, I have decent showdown value with Ace-high but no strong draws. The Small Blind checked, which likely indicates weakness, but betting here would mostly fold out hands I already beat and get called by better hands like pairs or stronger Aces. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to see a free turn card to try and improve or re-evaluate.

I am in the Small Blind with middle pair (sevens) on a board of 7d Jc 3s 3h. The board is paired, which reduces the value of my hand slightly as the opponent could potentially have a trey or a higher pair, and I have already checked the flop. As the first to act, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and reach showdown without overcommitting with a marginal hand. It also allows me to induce potential bluffs from the opponent.

With Ace-high on a paired 7-J-3-3 board, I likely still have the best hand after my opponent checks twice. Betting now might fold out worse high-card hands like King- or Queen-high, but could get called by any pair (7x, small pocket pairs) that currently beats me. Checking behind continues to control the pot size, takes a free river card, and preserves my showdown value without risking chips unnecessarily.

The hand has been checked through on the flop and turn, indicating that neither player has shown significant strength. My pair of 7s is a marginal holding with some showdown value. Given the board texture and the passive play thus far, betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (such as any Jack or a better pocket pair), whereas checking allows me to reach a cheap showdown and evaluate the opponent's strength if they choose to bet.

With Ace-high on a final board of 7-J-3-3-4, and the opponent checking all three streets, my Ace-high has significant showdown value. Betting here would likely only get called by hands that beat me, such as a pair, while folding out hands I already beat like King-high or Queen-high. Checking behind allows me to get to a free showdown and win the pot when my opponent has missed entirely.
Being on the button with 5c 6c, I have a playable hand that performs well in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to steal the blinds and take the initiative in the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with A6o, facing a 3BB open raise from the Button. A6o is a weak offsuit Ace that plays very poorly out of position. Calling would leave me OOP in a bloated pot with a hand that is often dominated. Given that the Big Blind is still left to act and has shown aggressive 3-betting tendencies in previous hands, 3-betting as a bluff is also unappealing here. The safest and most +EV play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Five offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 3x the big blind. This is a weak hand to defend with out of position, even against a button open. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to avoid being in a difficult spot post-flop.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand. From the button 3-handed, I prefer to open tighter offsuit kings like K8o+ and suited kings. Folding is the best choice to preserve chips and avoid marginal situations post-flop.

J2o is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. Although it's folded to me in the Small Blind, this hand has poor playability and strength. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than risking chips with a bottom-tier starting hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand even for three-handed play.
